answer:
**Manusmṛti** (Book 1) features catalogues of progenitors and created beings as part of its cosmogonic prologue, grounding social *dharma* in divine origins. A key stretch enumerates the ten "lords of progeny" created by Manu (Viraj), followed by their further creations:

> Then I, desiring to create progeny, performed the most difficult *tapas* and created at the beginning ten great sages, the lords of progeny. Marichi, Atri, Angiras, Pulastya, Pulaha, Kratu, Prachetas, Vasishtha, Bhrigu, and also Narada.[^1]  
> These beings of great splendor created seven other Manus, the gods and the orders of gods, and great seers of immeasurable power.[^2]

This cataloguing **authorises** the ensuing *dharmaśāstra*: by tracing laws back through Manu, sages, and cosmic creation, it establishes textual and social authority from the Self-Existent One's order, legitimising varna duties (e.g., later in ch.1). It also **instructs** on hierarchical origins, differentiating beings by birth-mode and function.[^3]

**Viṣṇu Smṛti** (opening of ch.1) opens with a metaphorical catalogue equating Viṣṇu's Boar avatar's body parts to Vedic sacrifice elements, followed by lists of created entities. The Boar stretch:

> He, the great ascetic, whose feet were the Vedas, whose tusks were the sacrificial post, whose teeth were the sacrifice, whose mouth was the fire-altar... Whose heart was the sacrificial fee, the great one of yoga and great mantras, Whose charming lips were the introductory rite, whose navel-whirl was the Pravargya ceremony; Whose path of movement was the various poetic meters, whose seat was the secret Upanishads...[^4]

This **authorises** *dharma* by embodying it in Viṣṇu himself—sacrifice is cosmogonic, supporting Earth (who asks for varna/ashrama laws). It **instructs** ritual symbolism while hymning the god; a later epithet-list by Earth reinforces divine authority.[^5]

**Widsith** (Old English Elegies, ch.14) is framed as the scop's boast, with extensive catalogues of rulers/peoples, travels, and retainers to showcase his wide experience. Prime stretch:

> \"Many men I have heard of who ruled over nations! ...Of these, Hwala was for a time the best, and Alexander the most powerful of all mankind... Attila ruled the Huns, Eormanric the Goths, Becca the Banings, the Burgundians Gifica. Caesar ruled the Greeks...\" (continuing ~40 rulers/tribes).[^6]

Cataloguing here **displays skill**: Widsith "unlocks his word-hoard" to recite lore, earning treasure; it boasts encyclopedic knowledge of heroic geography/history, elevating the poet amid a lost pagan world (evoking faint **mourning** for transient glory). Unlike the smṛtis' cosmic legitimacy, it performs oral artistry for patronage.[^7]

| Text | Catalogue Type | Primary Function(s) |
|------|----------------|---------------------|
| Manusmṛti 1 | Progenitors/beings | Authorising, instructing |
| Viṣṇu Smṛti opening | Divine body/creation | Authorising, instructing |
| Widsith | Rulers/tribes/retainers | Displaying skill (w/ mourning undertones) |

[^1]: Manusmṛti, ch.1, [p-23fa2c](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/01/translation/#p-23fa2c).
[^2]: Manusmṛti, ch.1, [p-9e656d](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/01/translation/#p-9e656d).
[^3]: Manusmṛti, ch.1, [p-1c8d86](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/01/translation/#p-1c8d86) (overviews *shāstra* contents).
[^4]: Viṣṇu Smṛti, ch.1, [paragraphs](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/01/translation/#p-979e7a#p-582b13) from p-979e7a to p-582b13.
[^5]: Viṣṇu Smṛti, ch.1, [p-e8a664](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/01/translation/#p-e8a664) (Earth's request); epithets span [p-4b7b81](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/01/translation/#p-4b7b81) to [p-cdc451](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/01/translation/#p-cdc451).
[^6]: Old English Elegies, ch.14 (*Widsith*), [p-545201](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/14-widsith-matholade-wordhord-onleac/translation/#p-545201).
[^7]: Old English Elegies, ch.14 (*Widsith*), [p-2f6bd3](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/14-widsith-matholade-wordhord-onleac/translation/#p-2f6bd3) (opening boast); [p-6bde05](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/14-widsith-matholade-wordhord-onleac/translation/#p-6bde05) (scop's role).
